---
# =====================================================================
#  Research highlight v3 — v2 structure + findings section (two-page)
#  Render:  quarto render epa-compliance-workshops_v3.qmd
#  Design system: _style.tex (colours, fonts, boxes, macros)
# =====================================================================
format:
  pdf:
    pdf-engine: pdflatex
    documentclass: article
    fontsize: 11pt
    geometry:
      - top=0.4in
      - bottom=0.45in
      - left=0.65in
      - right=0.65in
      - includehead
      - headheight=52pt
      - headsep=10pt
      - includefoot
      - footskip=24pt
    include-in-header:
      - file: ../../../formats/research-highlight/_style.tex
      - text: |
          % --- Running masthead + footer (logo, label, rule, page number) ---
          \usepackage{fancyhdr}
          \renewcommand{\headrulewidth}{0pt}
          \renewcommand{\footrulewidth}{0pt}
          \fancypagestyle{brief}{%
            \fancyhf{}%
            \fancyhead[L]{%
              \begin{minipage}[c]{0.62\textwidth}\raggedright\color{ink}\includegraphics[height=0.45in]{../../../formats/logos/eil-logo-maroon.png}\end{minipage}\hfill%
              \begin{minipage}[c]{0.36\textwidth}\raggedleft\fontsize{8.25pt}{10pt}\selectfont\bfseries\color{accentred}\textls[200]{\MakeUppercase{Research Highlight}}\end{minipage}%
              \par\vspace{2pt}%
              \noindent\textcolor{ink}{\rule{\textwidth}{2.2pt}}%
            }%
            \fancyfoot[C]{%
              \color{warmrule}\rule{\textwidth}{0.7pt}\par\vspace{3pt}%
              \hbox to\textwidth{%
                \color{faint}\fontsize{7.4pt}{9pt}\selectfont
                \hfill
                \textls[40]{\MakeUppercase{Page \thepage}}%
              }%
            }%
          }
          \pagestyle{brief}
    keep-tex: true
---

```{=latex}
% ---------------------------------------------------------------------
% TITLE + CITATION  (masthead now repeats in the page header)
% ---------------------------------------------------------------------
\brieftitle{EPA Compliance Workshops Don't Reduce Violations}
\briefcitation{Paul J. Ferraro \& Jay P. Shimshack \textperiodcentered{} \textit{Journal of Policy Analysis and Management}, 2026 \textperiodcentered{} \href{https://doi.org/10.1002/pam.70056}{doi.org/10.1002/pam.70056}}

\vspace{3pt}

% override body font size for this document
\fontsize{10.5pt}{13.5pt}\selectfont

% ---------------------------------------------------------------------
% BOTTOM LINE
% ---------------------------------------------------------------------
\begin{bottomline}
{\fontsize{11pt}{15pt}\selectfont
\textbf{Offering polluters free help to follow the rules doesn't make them more likely to follow them.} Researchers studied hundreds of Ohio facilities offered free compliance workshops and found no drop in violations. The program looked like a success at first glance --- but that improvement was a trend already underway, not an effect of the workshops.
}
\end{bottomline}

\vspace{8pt}

% ---------------------------------------------------------------------
% BACKGROUND
% ---------------------------------------------------------------------
\sectionhead{Background}
\noindent Environmental enforcement has long centered on penalties: inspect facilities, catch violations, and levy fines. \textbf{Compliance assistance} takes a different approach. Rather than punishing facilities that break the rules, regulators help them follow the rules in the first place --- through free workshops, guidance, and technical support --- on the premise that many violations, especially at smaller operations, stem from confusion or limited expertise rather than deliberate corner-cutting. It is cheaper than litigation, less adversarial, and has grown more popular across administrations. Yet whether the help actually changes behavior had rarely been tested. New research puts it to the test, studying an Ohio program that offered free assistance to hundreds of small water-treatment facilities with a recent history of violations.

\vspace{8pt}

% ---------------------------------------------------------------------
% WHAT THE STUDY FOUND
% ---------------------------------------------------------------------
\sectionhead{What the study found}
\noindent To measure the program's effect, the researchers compared facilities that had received the workshops with otherwise similar facilities that hadn't yet been reached, using the program's gradual roll-out across the state. They tracked both regulatory violations and the actual pollution facilities discharged.

\vspace{4pt}

\noindent Looking only at violations before and after the program rolled out, it looks like a clear success: violations fell by about half. But that drop had begun \textit{before} the workshops started, and it continued at the same pace afterward, among assisted and unassisted facilities alike. That is the misunderstanding the study corrects: by stripping out that underlying trend, it shows the workshops made no real difference to violations or pollution.

\vspace{8pt}

% ---------------------------------------------------------------------
% FIGURE — centred, stand-alone
% ---------------------------------------------------------------------
\begin{center}
\begin{minipage}{0.70\linewidth}
\figtitle{Violations per facility, 2014--2019}{Source: Ferraro \& Shimshack (2026).}
\includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{../figures/fig1-v2.png}
\end{minipage}
\end{center}

\vspace{8pt}

% ---------------------------------------------------------------------
% TAKEAWAY
% ---------------------------------------------------------------------
\sectionhead{Takeaway}
\noindent The finding also points to why the workshops fell short. Compliance assistance rests on the idea that facilities break the rules because they don't fully understand them. For these wastewater plants, that wasn't the case --- extra training and guidance changed little, a sign that their violations had other roots. A program that solves a problem facilities don't actually have was never likely to work.

\vspace{4pt}

\noindent The stakes are practical, not academic. Buoyed by the apparent success, the EPA had moved to expand the program to other states, spreading an approach that the evidence here says does not work. Expanding an ineffective program only multiplies its cost: wasted money, pollution that is never curbed, and communities that never see cleaner water. The lesson is not that assistance can never help; this is one program, and small benefits can't be ruled out. The point is that such programs should be rigorously tested before they replace tools already known to work.

```
